topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion. My heart gave a sudden, heavy thump against my ribs. I clicked it, and the words swam before my eyes. The headline was mine—my carefully crafted analysis of urban planning—but the byline belonged to a stranger. A name I didn't recognize, a face I’d never seen in my professional circle. I reread the text, the familiar arguments suddenly feeling alien, belonging to someone else’s voice. I stood up so quickly my chair scraped loudly against the floor. My hands were slick with a cold sweat. I walked to the window, staring out at the bustling street, every sound—the horns, the chatter—seeming amplified and distant. Who had done this? A prank? A mistake? The certainty of my own work evaporated, leaving a hollow space where confidence used to reside.

[story 2]

I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting off my glasses. The email had landed like a physical blow. It detailed the publication, the date, the content—all mine. Except for the signature. A different name. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I started tracing the lines of the text with a trembling finger, searching for any clue, any misplaced word that might betray the deception. My jaw tightened, muscles locking up beneath my skin. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest. The room tilted slightly. It felt as if the ground beneath me had shifted, leaving me unsteady. I picked up my phone, my fingers clumsy as I scrolled through contacts, searching for anyone who might know this secret, but the silence in the apartment was deafening, filled only by the frantic drumming inside my own chest.

[story 3]

When I opened the digital file, the initial shock was a strange numbness. My vision blurred momentarily. The article was there, undeniably mine, yet the attribution was a complete fabrication. I blinked several times, trying to force the information to settle, but it refused to cohere. My throat felt tight, constricted as if I couldn't draw a full breath. I pushed the laptop away, the sound of the closing lid echoing unnervingly in the quiet office. I began pacing the length of the room, my steps uneven, my gaze darting from one wall to the next, searching for an explanation that simply wasn't there. Every logical pathway seemed blocked; the pieces of my reality were jumbled, refusing to snap into place. I stopped abruptly, gripping the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white, utterly unable to focus on anything but the unsettling emptiness spreading through my core.
